schema_notes: Variable precision: each entry's 'precision' field tells the renderer how confident the date is. Values: 'day' (specific date), 'month', 'year', 'period' (spans multiple years), 'decade'. Locations are physical journeys; the Isra/Mi'raj is included with a 'spiritual' category since sources differ on whether it was physical.
sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (Guillaume tr.)
Martin Lings, Muhammad: His Life Based on the Earliest Sources
Safi-ur-Rahman Mubarakpuri, Ar-Raheeq Al-Makhtum (The Sealed Nectar)

journey.event: Born in Mecca to the Banu Hashim clan of Quraysh, in the Year of the Elephant. His father Abdullah had died months earlier on a trade journey.
Sent to live with the desert wet-nurse Halima of Banu Sa'd, following Meccan custom. Spent his early childhood among the Bedouin.
Returned to his mother Amina in Mecca.
Travelled with his mother Amina to visit relatives in Yathrib (Medina). On the return journey she fell ill and died at Abwa. He was now an orphan in both parents.
Brought back to Mecca by his nurse Umm Ayman, taken into the household of his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib.
Death of his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib. Guardianship passed to his uncle Abu Talib, who would protect him for the next four decades.
First journey outside Arabia. Travelled with Abu Talib's trading caravan to Bosra in Byzantine Syria. Tradition reports an encounter with the Christian monk Bahira.
Returned to Mecca with the caravan.
Second journey to Syria, this time leading a trading caravan for the wealthy widow Khadija bint Khuwaylid. His honesty in the venture earned him the title Al-Amin (the Trustworthy).
Married Khadija bint Khuwaylid in Mecca. The marriage would last 25 years until her death.
Mediated the dispute over placement of the Black Stone during the rebuilding of the Kaaba. The Quraysh clans had been on the verge of war over the honour.
Began periodic spiritual retreats in the Cave of Hira, a small grotto in the mountain overlooking Mecca, withdrawing for nights of solitude and reflection.
First revelation. While in retreat in the Cave of Hira, the angel Jibril appeared and commanded him to recite. The opening verses of Surah al-Alaq were revealed.
Returned home shaken to Khadija, who became the first to believe in his prophethood. Began three years of private preaching to family and close friends.
Began public preaching in Mecca after the verse 'Proclaim openly what you are commanded' was revealed. Faced immediate opposition from the Quraysh leadership.
Sent a group of his followers to seek refuge in Abyssinia (Ethiopia) under the Christian Negus, escaping persecution. He himself remained in Mecca.
The Quraysh imposed a social and economic boycott on Banu Hashim. Muhammad and his clan were confined to a narrow valley quarter of Mecca for nearly three years, suffering hunger.
The Year of Sorrow. Within months he lost both his wife Khadija and his protector uncle Abu Talib. The Quraysh now moved against him openly.
Travelled to Ta'if seeking refuge and a new audience for his message. The leaders of Thaqif rejected him; he was driven out and stoned by the town's youths, leaving him bloodied.
Returned to Mecca under the protection of Mut'im ibn Adi after his clan's protection had effectively lapsed.

The Isra and Mi'raj — the Night Journey from Mecca to Jerusalem and the ascent through the heavens. Sources differ on whether this was a physical or spiritual journey; included here as it is foundational to the sirah narrative.
Met six men from Yathrib (Medina) at the Aqaba pass during the pilgrimage season. They accepted his message and returned home as the first Medinan Muslims.
First Pledge of Aqaba. Twelve men from Medina pledged allegiance to him at Aqaba and asked for a teacher to be sent back with them.
Second Pledge of Aqaba. Seventy-three men and two women from Medina pledged to defend him as their own. The way was now open for migration.
Slipped out of Mecca by night with Abu Bakr, narrowly escaping a Quraysh assassination plot. The Hijra (migration) had begun.
Hid for three nights in the Cave of Thawr with Abu Bakr while Quraysh search parties combed the area. A spider's web and a dove's nest at the cave mouth concealed them.
Arrived at Quba after travelling north along an unusual desert route. Stayed several days and founded the first mosque of Islam — Masjid Quba.
Entered Yathrib, soon to be renamed Madinat al-Nabi (the City of the Prophet). The Hijra became Year 1 of the Islamic calendar.
Built the Prophet's Mosque (Masjid al-Nabawi) on land purchased from two orphans. Drafted the Constitution of Medina, a pact among Muslims, Jews, and pagan tribes of the city.
Battle of Badr. Led roughly 313 Muslims against a Quraysh army of about 1,000. The unexpected Muslim victory secured the survival of the new community. Badr is named in the Quran.
Returned to Medina after Badr. Expelled the Banu Qaynuqa, a Jewish tribe of Medina, after a breach of the city pact.
Battle of Uhud. A Quraysh army of 3,000 marched on Medina. The Muslims initially gained the upper hand but were routed when archers abandoned their position. Muhammad was wounded.
Returned wounded to Medina. Expelled the Banu Nadir, another Jewish tribe of Medina, after their alleged plot against him.

Battle of the Trench (al-Khandaq). A confederate army of 10,000 besieged Medina. On the advice of Salman al-Farisi, a defensive trench was dug along the city's exposed flank. The siege failed after a month of stalemate and bad weather.

Set out for Mecca with 1,400 unarmed pilgrims intending to perform Umrah. Halted at Hudaybiyyah on the edge of the Meccan sanctuary. Negotiated the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah with Quraysh — a ten-year truce.
Marched against the Jewish stronghold of Khaybar, an oasis north of Medina. The fortress fell after a siege; survivors were allowed to remain as cultivators paying tribute.
Performed the 'compensatory Umrah' (Umrat al-Qada) under the terms of Hudaybiyyah, his first return to Mecca since the Hijra. Quraysh withdrew from the city for three days as agreed.

Conquest of Mecca. After Quraysh allies broke the truce, marched on Mecca with 10,000 men. The city surrendered with almost no resistance. He cleared the Kaaba of idols and proclaimed a general amnesty.

Battle of Hunayn. Marched against the Hawazin and Thaqif tribes who had gathered to attack. Initially routed by an ambush in the valley, the Muslim army rallied around him and won decisively. Named in the Quran.
Besieged Ta'if for nearly three weeks following Hunayn. The city did not fall; he lifted the siege and returned to Mecca. Ta'if would convert peacefully a year later.
Distributed the spoils of Hunayn at Ji'ranah and performed Umrah, then returned north to Medina.
Returned to Medina after an absence of several months. Began receiving delegations from across Arabia as tribes formally embraced Islam — the 'Year of Delegations' followed.

Tabuk expedition. Led 30,000 men over 700 km north toward the Byzantine frontier in response to reports of a Roman mobilisation. No battle took place; he stayed twenty days, secured treaties with northern tribes, and returned. His last military expedition.
Returned to Medina from Tabuk. Spent the next year and a half receiving delegations and consolidating the Muslim polity across Arabia.
Set out from Medina with around 100,000 pilgrims for the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hijjat al-Wada'). Performed the rites of Hajj and established their final form for all Muslims to follow.

Delivered the Farewell Sermon from atop Mount Arafat to a vast assembly of pilgrims. The Quranic verse 'Today I have perfected your religion for you' (5:3) is traditionally said to have been revealed here.
Returned to Medina after the Farewell Pilgrimage. Within weeks, the final illness began.
Died on Monday, 12 Rabi al-Awwal 11 AH (8 June 632 CE), in the chamber of his wife A'isha. Buried on the spot where he died — the site is now within the Prophet's Mosque.

confidence.disclosure: The Prophet's biography is the best-documented life in pre-modern Islamic history. Ibn Ishaq's Sira (d. 767), al-Waqidi's Maghazi (d. 823), Ibn Sa'd's Tabaqat, and the canonical hadith collections provide day- and month-level dating for major events. The Hijra, Badr, Uhud, Hudaybiyya, and the Conquest of Mecca are precisely dated. Earlier Meccan chronology is less certain.
